description: Common construction subcontract clause patterns. Claude reads this before analyzing. Match fingerprint keywords to the contract text.

patterns.plain_english: You only get paid when the GC gets paid by the owner — no fixed deadline.
patterns.what_it_means: If the owner is slow, disputes a draw, or goes bankrupt, your payment is frozen indefinitely. No clock is ticking in your favor.
patterns.pushback_template: Request a pay-when-paid backstop: GC must pay within 45 days regardless of owner payment status.

statutes.GA: O.C.G.A. § 13-11-4 (Prompt Pay Act — 10 days after GC receipt)
statutes.CA: Civil Code § 8814 (7 days after GC receipt)

patterns.plain_english: If the owner never pays the GC, you never get paid — your payment is a true condition, not just timing.
patterns.what_it_means: Owner insolvency or dispute = you absorb the loss. This is the most dangerous payment clause in construction.
patterns.pushback_template: Pay-if-paid clauses require explicit 'condition precedent' language to be enforceable. Challenge the language and request a backstop.

statutes.GA: Georgia courts require explicit condition precedent language — ambiguous clauses construed as pay-when-paid
statutes.CA: Cal. Civil Code § 8122 — pay-if-paid unenforceable for licensed contractors on private works

patterns.fingerprint: retainage project closeout final completion owner acceptance
patterns.plain_english: Your 10% retainage is held until the entire project closes — not when your scope is done.
patterns.what_it_means: You could finish your scope in month 3 of a 14-month project and wait over a year to collect money you already earned.
patterns.pushback_template: Push for retainage release within 60 days of your substantial completion, not project closeout.

statutes.GA: O.C.G.A. § 13-10-80 — must release within 60 days of sub's substantial completion
statutes.CA: Cal. Civil Code § 8812 — public works retainage rules

patterns.fingerprint: 48 hours notice extra work change order
patterns.plain_english: You must give written notice of any extra work within 48 hours or you waive recovery rights.
patterns.what_it_means: In real field conditions, a changed condition on a Friday afternoon means you lose recovery rights by Sunday if you miss the window.
patterns.pushback_template: Request 7 calendar days (or 5 business days minimum) for written extra work notice. Remove 'time is of the essence' from this clause.

patterns.plain_english: GC can deduct backcharges from your payments at any time without prior written notice.
patterns.what_it_means: You could receive a payment 30% lower than expected with no advance warning and no ability to dispute before the deduction happens.
patterns.pushback_template: Request 5-day advance written notice with documentation before any backcharge deduction. Without notice, backcharge is waived.

patterns.plain_english: You are exposed to per-day liquidated damages for project delays that may include delays caused by others.
patterns.what_it_means: LD clauses without a scope cap can expose you to charges for delays caused by the owner, GC, or other trades — not just your work.
patterns.pushback_template: Request LD exposure capped at your contract value and limited to delays directly caused by your scope. Ask to see the prime contract LD clause before signing.

patterns.fingerprint: indemnify defend hold harmless any and all claims
patterns.plain_english: You must defend and pay for any claims arising from the project, including claims caused by the GC or owner.
patterns.what_it_means: Broad indemnity with no carve-out for GC negligence transfers the GC's legal costs and liability onto you. 'Defend' means you pay legal fees upfront, not just damages.
patterns.pushback_template: Request mutual indemnity limited to each party's own acts. Remove GC negligence from subcontractor's indemnity obligation.

statutes.GA: Georgia HB 137 (2022) / O.C.G.A. § 13-8-2 — requiring sub to indemnify GC for GC's own negligence is VOID

patterns.plain_english: GC can terminate the contract at any time for any reason, and you only receive payment for work already completed — no lost profit.
patterns.what_it_means: If you mobilized, ordered materials, and scheduled crews, a termination-for-convenience clause means you absorb all sunk costs with no recovery for anticipated profit.
patterns.pushback_template: Request payment for completed work PLUS reasonable overhead and profit on the terminated portion. Minimum: demobilization costs and material restocking fees.
